summaries:
The team is planning a comprehensive EKS migration from us-east-1 to us-west-2. The current production environment runs on the molt-prod-east cluster in us-east-1, serving the application at https://app-prod-v1.us-east-1.example.com, where the v1 prefix denotes API version rather than environment. Deployments are managed through ArgoCD with a 3-minute sync interval. The production workload uses a Postgres 14 database on RDS running on a db.r6g.large instance, and a critical decision was made to maintain this configuration rather than migrating to Aurora Serverless v2 due to unacceptable Lambda cold-start latency of 3.2s at p99, which would severely impact the authentication path.

The target region us-west-2 was selected over us-west-1 for two key reasons: it offers four availability zones compared to three, and it provides access to Bedrock, which will be required for an upcoming RAG service. The new cluster will be deployed directly on EKS version 1.29, skipping the interim upgrade path and gaining support through 2027. Infrastructure will be provisioned using CDK, leveraging existing modules in lib/eks-stack.ts with the new region configured via stage variables.

The current IAM configuration uses pod role arn:aws:iam::492372116065:role/eks-pod-prod-east, which grants RDS connectivity, S3 read/write access to the molt-prod-data bucket, and SecretsManager read permissions for resources under molt-prod/*. The API Gateway (ID a1b2c3d4e5) serves traffic through the custom domain api.example.com with WAF rules at version 3. IRSA configuration will function identically in the new region, requiring only the one-time addition of an OIDC provider in the target account.

Cross-region connectivity has been designed to avoid VPC peering complexity, instead relying on public S3 endpoints with bucket policies. Cross-region replication operates asynchronously with a p95 replication lag of approximately 200ms. Secrets Manager replication is enabled for the project namespace with propagation times under 60 seconds. DNS management uses a global Route53 hosted zone with weighted routing for the api.example.com record and attached health checks. Existing Lambda functions will continue invoking the API Gateway endpoint transparently during the transition, with timeouts increased to 30s on cold-start sensitive paths.

A decommissioning thread labeled old-cluster-decom has been established to coordinate the shutdown of the us-east-1 infrastructure. The plan includes draining workloads, snapshotting RDS, exporting ArgoCD configurations, and tearing down the VPC. As a precautionary measure, daily RDS snapshots will be taken for 14 days before any teardown activities begin. Cost projections show a temporary spike of approximately $1200 per month during the dual-running window, followed by savings of $3400 per month below baseline once the eastern cluster is fully decommissioned. Integration testing is running against a smaller replica environment in the dev account, achieving approximately 85% line coverage on migration scripts. An open discussion in the #infra Slack channel is addressing shared NAT gateway costs, with Thomson tagged for review. URL conventions have been documented in repo/docs/url-conventions.md to ensure consistency across environments.

This conversation documents a comprehensive cross-region migration from an existing east region deployment to a new us-west-2 infrastructure. The team has established a new EKS cluster named molt-prod-west running version 1.29, deliberately skipping version 1.28. The cluster uses IAM role arn:aws:iam::492372116065:role/eks-pod-prod-west with the same permission scope as the east region but targeting us-west-2 resources. A new S3 bucket molt-prod-data-west-2 has been created with active Cross-Region Replication from the existing molt-prod-data bucket, showing p95 replication lag around 200ms. The infrastructure leverages public S3 endpoints with bucket policies rather than VPC peering for cross-region traffic.

The migration includes several key services and configurations. Bedrock has been enabled in us-west-2 with anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-v1:0 as the default model for the RAG service, with appropriate IAM permissions added to the pod role. A new API Gateway identified as w8x9y0z1q2 has been deployed, which will eventually serve api.example.com once the cutover completes. The team has also initiated a new RAG service deployment tracked under the thread rag-service-deploy, configured with 3 replicas, bge-large as the embedded model, and pgvector on the new west RDS instance as the vector store. DNS management uses Route53's global hosted zone with weighted routing and health checks attached to the api.example.com record.

The migration follows a phased cutover plan spanning six weeks. Phase 1 begins next week with 5% shadow traffic routed through API Gateway weighted routing. Phase 2 occurs 14 days later with 50/50 traffic distribution, and Phase 3 at 30 days from start moves 100% of traffic to west and decommissions the east region. The rollback strategy is straightforward: reset weighted routing to 100% east while keeping ArgoCD running in west but receiving zero traffic, with the RDS replica maintaining synchronization. Existing Lambda functions remain unaffected by the transition as they continue invoking the API Gateway endpoint transparently, though timeouts have been increased to 30s on cold-start sensitive paths.

The team has initiated an authentication system rebuild under the tracking label `auth-rebuild-design`, structured in four phases: schema design, token issuance, validation middleware, and cutover. The core architectural decision was to adopt JWT with HS256 signing over OAuth-only, driven by the need for stateless validation at the edge to support a peak load of 12k requests per second without session-store roundtrips. The token strategy uses 15-minute access tokens paired with 7-day refresh tokens, with the JWT signing key stored in SSM Parameter Store at /molt/prod/jwt-signing-key, encrypted with KMS and rotated quarterly.

The existing authentication endpoint is /api/v1/auth/login, which currently issues a cookie named molt_session with HttpOnly and SameSite=Lax attributes. The JWT payload includes standard claims (sub for user_id, iss as auth.example.com, aud as api.example.com, exp, iat, jti as UUID) plus custom claims for amr (set to ['mfa'] when multi-factor authentication is passed) and tier (free/pro/enterprise). The SameSite=Lax setting was chosen because the application doesn't rely on cross-site POSTs for authentication flows, making the stricter Strict policy unnecessary.

Refresh token security employs a sliding window rotation strategy where each refresh operation issues a new refresh token and blacklists the old one in a Redis short-TTL set. The blacklist uses the existing molt-cache-prod Redis cluster (cluster mode, 3 shards, Multi-AZ) under the keyspace auth:refresh:blacklist:* with an 8-day TTL. When refresh operations fail, the system returns a 401 status with a WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token" header, prompting the client to redirect to /login. If Redis becomes unavailable, the blacklist check fails open (accepting the refresh token) while triggering a paging alert, with the option to switch to an in-memory fallback for brief outages. The rate-limit store also uses molt-cache-prod, which is confirmed to be highly available with Multi-AZ deployment.

Logging follows a tiered approach: INFO level for successful authentication paths, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures, all in JSON-structured format. Audit logs are retained for 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier to meet compliance requirements. Error messages support internationalization with keys for English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. The testing strategy includes unit tests on validation middleware achieving 95% coverage and end-to-end tests via Playwright covering the complete login, refresh, and revoke flow. A known Safari 16 issue with HttpOnly cookies in private mode is addressed with a fallback to sessionStorage accompanied by a security warning in the UI. A penetration test is planned with Cobalt once a staging deployment with the full token flow is available, with one outstanding security review item already resolved regarding the high availability of the rate-limit Redis store.

The team has moved from design into implementation of the authentication token issuance system, renaming the thread from `auth-rebuild-design` to `auth-token-issuance` to reflect this transition. The schema work is complete, and development has begun on the new token issuance endpoint at /api/v2/auth/token. This endpoint accepts POST requests with username, password, and mfa_code, returning both access and refresh tokens while setting a molt_session cookie.

A critical security decision was made to support TOTP-only multi-factor authentication via authenticator apps, explicitly excluding SMS-based MFA due to interception risks and US carrier reliability concerns for a financial application. For SSO users, the system will trust the SAML assertion's amr claim to indicate that MFA has already been enforced at the identity provider layer, avoiding redundant prompts. The JWT signing infrastructure has been updated with a new key at /molt/prod/jwt-signing-key-v2, encrypted with KMS multi-region key mrk-1234abcd5678efgh, while the old key is being retained for a seven-day grace period.

The molt_session cookie uses SameSite=Lax, deemed sufficient since no critical cross-site POST operations exist for authentication.

This conversation covers the onboarding and pipeline design for a new customer, Acme Corp, with Jane Smith serving as the primary contact at jane.smith@acmecorp.io in her role as Director of Data Engineering. Procurement closed yesterday, and the team is now working through technical implementation details under the tracking thread acme-pipeline-design, which includes subtasks for schema mapping, DAG authoring, and monitoring.

The team decided to use AWS MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) instead of self-hosted Argo Workflows, primarily because MWAA allows Acme to inherit SOC 2 compliance through AWS's attestation, satisfying their SOC 2 requirement without requiring separate attestation. The managed service also reduces operational burden. Infrastructure setup includes the S3 bucket s3://acme-intake-prod-492372 with KMS encryption, versioning enabled, and a lifecycle policy transitioning data to Glacier after 90 days. Acme's data volume is approximately 40GB per day with peaks at 80GB, consisting mostly of transactional CSVs and some Avro files, totaling around 12TB per year at steady state.

The service level agreement specifies 6-hour data freshness and 99.5% uptime under the standard tier without premium add-ons. Compliance requirements include handling PII data (emails and phone numbers) with tokenization required at ingest, and Acme needs SOC 2 Type II attestation by Q3. Schema drift is managed through Great Expectations validations on every DAG run, with schema changes triggering Slack alerts and pausing downstream DAGs. The backfill strategy uses DAG-level catchup=False, with manual triggering for explicit date ranges; Acme's historical data required a 30-day backfill completed over a weekend.

Source-side changes are handled through a webhook at /internal/acme/source-change that pauses affected DAGs and requires human acknowledgment to resume. A separate MWAA small environment in the dev account serves as the test environment, mirroring the production DAG layout with a synthetic data generator running nightly. Cost management includes weekly internal reviews in the #data-pipelines channel with costs tagged customer:acme for attribution. Cross-team dependencies involve the platform team owning the underlying MWAA service while the data team owns Acme-specific DAGs, with breaking changes reviewed via pull requests. Two open low-priority tickets address schema drift alert tuning due to high false-positive rates and Macie cost optimization to reduce scanning frequency from hourly to twice daily. The runbook is located at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md and covers on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift response, and cost spike response, though these runbooks remain internal while Acme maintains a customer-facing status page at status.acme.example.com.

metas.summary:
The session focused on standing up the new us-west-2 EKS cluster and finalizing the migration plan. The new cluster `molt-prod-west` is now live with EKS 1.29, confirming the decision to skip the interim 1.28 upgrade. The pod IAM role `arn:aws:iam::492372116065:role/eks-pod-prod-west` mirrors the east permissions but targets west-region resources. A new S3 bucket `molt-prod-data-west-2` was created with cross-region replication from the existing `molt-prod-data` bucket, maintaining the same ~200ms p95 replication lag. A new API Gateway `w8x9y0z1q2` was provisioned to eventually replace the east gateway once DNS cutover occurs.

Bedrock was enabled in us-west-2 with the default model `anthropic.claude-sonnet-4-v1:0` for the upcoming RAG service. A new sub-thread `rag-service-deploy` was established to track the RAG service deployment, which will run 3 replicas using the bge-large embedding model and pgvector on the new west RDS instance. The existing Lambda functions will continue invoking the API Gateway endpoint transparently during the transition, with timeouts bumped to 30s on cold-start sensitive paths.

The cutover plan was formalized into three phases: Phase 1 shadows 5% of traffic to west via API Gateway weighted routing (starting next week), Phase 2 moves to 50/50 split (+14 days), and Phase 3 completes the migration at 100% west (+30 days from start), totaling a 6-week migration window. Rollback strategy is straightforward: reset weighted routing to 100% east while keeping the west ArgoCD deployment running but receiving zero traffic. The decommissioning thread was renamed from `old-cluster-decom` to `east-region-decom` for clarity.

Infrastructure details were confirmed: CDK deployment via existing modules in `lib/eks-stack.ts`, no VPC peering needed (public S3 endpoints with bucket policies), Secrets Manager replication enabled with 60s propagation, IRSA working identically in west after adding the OIDC provider, and Route53 weighted routing on the global hosted zone with health checks. Integration tests are running against a smaller west copy in the dev account with ~85% line coverage. Cost projections remain unchanged: temporary $1,200/month spike during dual-running, then $3,400/month reduction post-decommission. The open Slack thread in #infra regarding shared NAT gateway costs remains tagged for Thomson's review.

metas.summary:
This session focused on designing a JWT-based authentication rebuild for a high-throughput API (12k rps peak). The core architectural decision was to use JWT with HS256 signing rather than OAuth-only, driven by the need for stateless validation at the edge cache layer. Token lifetimes were set at 15 minutes for access tokens and 7 days for refresh tokens, with a sliding-window refresh strategy that issues new refresh tokens on each refresh and blacklists old ones in Redis.

The implementation leverages existing infrastructure: the JWT signing key lives in SSM Parameter Store (`/molt/prod/jwt-signing-key`), encrypted with KMS and rotated quarterly. The refresh token blacklist uses a dedicated keyspace (`auth:refresh:blacklist:*`) in the existing `molt-cache-prod` Redis cluster (cluster mode, 3 shards, Multi-AZ) with an 8-day TTL. If Redis goes down, the system fails open (accepts refresh tokens) but triggers a paging alert, with an option to fall back to in-memory storage for short outages. The current login endpoint is `/api/v1/auth/login`, and the session cookie is named `molt_session` with HttpOnly and SameSite=Lax attributes.

The JWT payload includes standard claims (sub, iss, aud, exp, iat, jti) plus custom fields for MFA status (amr) and user tier (free/pro/enterprise). Issuer is `auth.example.com` and audience is `api.example.com`. Error handling returns 401 with `WWW-Authenticate: Bearer error="invalid_token"` headers on refresh failures, prompting client-side redirect to `/login`. Logging is JSON-structured with INFO for success, WARN for refresh failures, and ERROR for signing failures. Audit logs are retained 90 days hot in CloudWatch and 7 years cold in S3 Glacier for compliance.

Browser compatibility includes a Safari 16 workaround for HttpOnly cookie issues in private mode, falling back to sessionStorage with a security warning. Error messages support i18n in English, Spanish, French, and Japanese. Testing coverage includes 95% unit test coverage on validation middleware and end-to-end Playwright tests for the full login/refresh/revoke flow. A pen-test engagement with Cobalt is planned once staging deployment is complete. The rate-limit Redis store was confirmed to be HA (backed by the same multi-AZ `molt-cache-prod` cluster), closing the final open security review item.

metas.summary:
This session focused on implementing the JWT token issuance endpoint at `/api/v2/auth/token`, which accepts POST requests with username, password, and optional MFA code, returning both access and refresh tokens while setting the `molt_session` cookie. The thread was renamed from `auth-rebuild-design` to `auth-token-issuance` to reflect the shift from design to implementation. A critical MFA decision was made: the system will support TOTP via authenticator apps only, with no SMS fallback due to interception risks and carrier reliability concerns for a financial application.

The JWT signing key was rotated to a new multi-region KMS key (`mrk-1234abcd5678efgh`), with the new parameter path `/molt/prod/jwt-signing-key-v2`. The old key is retained for a 7-day grace period. For SSO users, MFA is handled at the IdP layer — the system trusts the SAML assertion's `amr` claim to indicate MFA completion and avoids double-prompting. All authentication events, including the `amr` value, IP, user agent, and success/failure status, are logged to the CloudWatch log group `/molt/prod/auth-audit`.

A token revocation endpoint was implemented at `POST /api/v2/auth/revoke`, which adds the JWT's `jti` to the Redis blacklist and revokes the entire refresh token chain. The refresh failure handling, logging levels, JWT payload structure, cookie attributes, browser compatibility workarounds, test coverage, internationalization support, Redis failover strategy, and audit log retention policies remain unchanged from the previous compaction. The final security review item — confirming the rate-limit Redis store is HA — was closed, as it's backed by the same multi-AZ `molt-cache-prod` cluster.

metas.summary:
This session covered the initial design and operational setup for a new customer data pipeline for Acme Corp. The primary contact is Jane Smith, Director of Data Engineering, with procurement closed yesterday. The core architectural decision was to use AWS MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) rather than self-hosted Argo Workflows, driven by SOC 2 compliance requirements and reduced operational burden—MWAA allows SOC 2 Type II attestation via AWS inheritance, which Acme needs by Q3.

Infrastructure setup includes a production S3 bucket (s3://acme-intake-prod-492372) with KMS encryption, versioning, and a 90-day lifecycle policy to Glacier. The pipeline handles approximately 40GB/day (peak 80GB), primarily transactional CSVs and some Avro, totaling around 12TB/year at steady state. The SLA is 6-hour data freshness with 99.5% uptime on the standard tier. PII (emails and phone numbers) is present and requires tokenization at ingest.

Operational patterns include schema drift detection via Great Expectations validations on every DAG run, with Slack alerts and automatic downstream DAG pausing on schema changes. Backfills are manual with explicit date ranges (catchup=False), and a 30-day historical backfill was completed over a weekend. Source-side changes are handled via a webhook to /internal/acme/source-change, which pauses affected DAGs and requires human acknowledgment to resume. A separate MWAA small environment in the dev account mirrors the prod DAG layout, with a synthetic data generator running nightly for testing.

Cost attribution uses the tag `customer:acme` with weekly internal reviews in #data-pipelines. Cross-team dependencies exist with the platform team, who own the underlying MWAA service while the data team owns Acme-specific DAGs; breaking changes require PR review. Two low-priority open tickets exist: tuning schema drift alerts (high false-positive rate) and optimizing Macie scans (reducing from hourly to twice daily). The runbook is located at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md and includes on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift response, and cost spikes. Acme has a customer-facing status page at status.acme.example.com.

metas.summary:
This session transitioned from active pipeline build to operational monitoring and cost optimization. The three DAGs (transactions, customers, products) are now running in production. The primary focus split into two new threads: establishing a monitoring strategy and driving down monthly costs. The monitoring approach uses Datadog for runtime metrics (DAG duration, task success/failure) and CloudWatch for AWS infrastructure (S3, MWAA, Lambda), with alerts routed through PagerDuty integration `acme-pipeline-prod`.

Current monthly cost is approximately $3400 (compute $2100, storage $700, transfer $400, Macie $200). The cost optimization target is to reduce this to under $2500/month, primarily by switching to a MWAA small environment (mw1.small) after 60 days of steady-state validation, which should save around $900/month. One SLA breach occurred on Friday when the source system held a 4-hour batch, exceeding the 6-hour freshness SLA. Acme acknowledged the source-side cause (maintenance window not communicated) and no penalty was applied. This incident was documented in the incident log.

All operational patterns from previous sessions remain in place: Great Expectations schema drift detection with automatic downstream DAG pausing, manual backfills with catchup=False, webhook-based source change notifications at /internal/acme/source-change, and a separate MWAA small environment in the dev account for testing with nightly synthetic data generation. The two low-priority open tickets (schema drift alert tuning and Macie cost optimization) remain active. The runbook at docs/runbooks/acme-pipeline.md includes on-call playbooks for SLA breaches, schema drift response, and cost spikes.
